quote: Okay. Sorry. Could you repeat that all one more time? So you're wanting to add on solar panels, and then what what else were you looking for?
stewart_read: Holland never delivered an intro. No company name, no legitimacy framing, no 'how are you doing today' pause. She went straight from 'Hi. This is Holland.' to intake. On a warm, self-motivated lead this didn't cost the booking — but the habit is the problem. On a cold or skeptical lead, the absence of a legitimacy anchor in the first 30 seconds is a trust deficit that compounds every subsequent ask.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that Christian's immediate, detailed ask signaled he already knew who he was calling and didn't need a formal intro. That read is plausible but not supported — Christian's first question ('Do you service Colorado Springs?') suggests he was not certain he had the right company.
coaching_implication: Pull this moment in the 1-on-1 and ask Holland: 'What's the first thing you said on this call?' The habit of skipping the intro is invisible to the rep until they hear it back. Anchor the codex intro template — even 10 seconds of legitimacy framing changes the customer's posture on cold leads.

quote: And I found your website or I found you from Enphase. So en do you Enphase. Mostly Enphase products that I'm looking for.
stewart_read: Christian arrived via Enphase referral and explicitly names Enphase microinverters as his product preference. Holland confirms without qualification: 'Yeah. Yep. We do have those.' The codex confirms Ion carries Tesla Powerwall as its only named battery brand and sells its own panel line — it is silent on Enphase microinverters. This is a potential product mismatch, not a minor verify gap. If Ion does not carry Enphase, Holland has set a product expectation the closer cannot fulfill.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may know from floor experience that Ion does carry Enphase products and the confirmation is accurate. If so, this is not a miss — but it still needs to be flagged for the closer so they can confirm the product fit at the proposal stage.
coaching_implication: Before the closer appointment, confirm with Ion's product team whether Enphase microinverters are in Ion's catalog. If yes, add Enphase to the codex's ion_offerings_beyond_setter_call section and coach Holland to flag the specific product preference in her handoff note rather than giving an unqualified 'you came to the right place.' If no, this is a critical handoff flag — the closer needs to reframe before Christian sees the proposal.

quote: Great. And then, yeah, the home looks great. I mean, you get just straight sunlight. And then I see is your solar system on the ground in the back?
stewart_read: Holland's use of satellite imagery to confirm the ground-mount system is the strongest trust-building moment on the call. She is not asking Christian to describe his property — she already knows it and is confirming. This signals competence and preparation, which is exactly the 'neighbor-like' energy the codex targets. Christian's immediate 'Yes. Mhmm. Okay.' is a trust confirmation, not just a factual acknowledgment.
coaching_implication: Reinforce this explicitly. Holland should know this move works and should use it consistently — especially on orphaned or expansion leads where the existing system is visible on satellite. The manager can use this moment as a positive anchor in the 1-on-1 before moving to the gaps.

quote: for for any of the programs or the incentives, you just need to have a credit score above six seventy. Is that the case for either of you?
stewart_read: Holland states the correct 670 threshold — matching the codex — and applies it to both title holders ('either of you'), which is the correct protocol given Jeanette is on the title. This is a clean qualifier execution. Noteworthy because SESSION18_fcbf33c1 shows a rep stating 650 instead of 670; Holland's execution here is schema-compliant and worth positive reinforcement.
coaching_implication: Call this out as a positive in the 1-on-1. Holland got the threshold right and applied it to both title holders without prompting. This is the correct move and should be named as such — positive reinforcement on protocol adherence is as important as correcting gaps.

quote: And then you're just with Colorado Springs Utilities, I'm assuming?
stewart_read: Holland assumes the utility provider rather than asking. Christian corrects her — he is with Mountain View, not Colorado Springs Utilities — and adds context about Mountain View being harder to work with on solar. The assumption is a minor efficiency gap on this call because Christian corrects it easily, but the habit of assuming utility provider rather than asking is a verify discipline issue. On a less forthcoming customer, the assumption could go uncorrected and produce a bad handoff.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have been using the satellite-confirmed address to infer the most common utility in that ZIP code. That is a reasonable inference, but the codex's verify section treats utility company as a required ask, not an inference.
coaching_implication: Small fix: replace 'you're just with Colorado Springs Utilities, I'm assuming?' with 'And who's your utility provider out there?' One word change, zero friction, eliminates the assumption habit. Worth a 30-second note in the 1-on-1.

quote: Last year, we bought so last year, we got another electric car and we put in a hot tub, which is part of the reason we need more panels. And we spent, $788 on electricity.
stewart_read: Christian volunteers two high-load additions — a second EV and a hot tub — as the explicit driver of his increased consumption. These are exactly the enthusiasm signals the codex flags (EV recently bought, hot tub added). Holland acknowledges with 'Yeah. Exactly.' and moves on. She does not anchor on the load-growth trajectory or use it to build curiosity about what the battery-plus-expansion design would look like for his specific usage pattern. On this lead, the bill flip is not the right tool — Christian is already self-motivated and non-cost-sensitive. But a single curiosity sentence about how the battery design changes when you're running two EVs and a hot tub would have deepened his investment in the appointment.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have correctly read that Christian didn't need emotional alignment and moved efficiently to scheduling. That read is well-supported — Christian explicitly says 'we're not looking at this from a cost payback type situation.' The coaching gap here is narrower than a full bill-anchor failure: it is one missed curiosity sentence, not a missed flip.
coaching_implication: Use this moment to teach the distinction between the bill flip (for cost-skeptical customers who need emotional alignment) and the curiosity sentence (for already-motivated customers who benefit from a specific hook into the appointment). A suggested rephrase: 'Two EVs and a hot tub — that's a completely different system design than your original install. That's exactly what the specialist will map out for you.' That sentence costs nothing and raises Christian's anticipation of the appointment.

quote: Could you do, like, 04:00? And would this be at the house, or would this be, somewhere or, you know, virtual?
stewart_read: Christian asks whether the appointment is in-home or virtual. Holland confirms in-home without asking whether that works for both Christian and Jeanette. The button-up confirms the time and the one-hour block, but does not include the 'not driving, at a computer' anchor from the codex — which is less relevant for an in-home visit but the 'is that fair' verbal handshake is also absent. The in-home format is appropriate given the existing ground-mount system needs physical inspection, but Holland should have confirmed Jeanette's availability for an in-home appointment specifically.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that Christian's 'we' language throughout the call ('we're spending,' 'we wanna have the battery') was sufficient confirmation that Jeanette would be present. That is a reasonable read but not a substitute for explicit confirmation.
coaching_implication: At the button-up, add one line: 'And Jeanette will be home Thursday at four as well?' This is the spouse-protocol discipline applied to an in-home appointment. The codex is explicit that co-title-holders must be present. Holland captured Jeanette's name and title status correctly — she just didn't close the loop on her presence at the appointment.

quote: I'll just wait on that usage from you, and then, yeah, we will see you next Thursday.
stewart_read: Holland closes the call with the appointment booked but the usage data still outstanding — she is 'waiting on' the spreadsheet Christian offered to send. This is a soft dependency: if Christian does not send the spreadsheet, the closer arrives without usage data. Holland does not set a deadline, does not confirm what format she needs, and does not tell Christian what happens if she doesn't receive it before Thursday. The booking is real but the data collection is unresolved.
alternative_hypothesis: Holland may have judged that Christian's offer to send a spreadsheet was reliable enough to leave open-ended. Christian is organized and detail-oriented — he tracks his usage in a spreadsheet, which is unusually thorough. The risk of non-delivery is lower here than on a typical lead. Still, a deadline or a 'if I don't hear from you by Tuesday I'll follow up' would protect the sit.
coaching_implication: Coach Holland to close the data loop before ending the call: 'If you can get that to me by Tuesday, I'll make sure the specialist has everything they need before Thursday.' This converts an open dependency into a committed timeline and protects the sit quality.
